title: Vintage Bar Stool
summary: A simple 2x2-legged bar stool with a slatted seat and a low backrest — straightforward joinery, built on pocket holes.
description:
There's no compound angle or curved leg here — this stool is straight cuts and pocket-hole joints, which makes it a good project if the double-beveled, splayed-leg style of bar stool feels like more than you want to take on right now. The backrest is what gives it its vintage look, built as its own small frame and joined to the seat assembly.

One detail worth not skipping: drilling the apron's pocket holes facing upward, before you attach the legs, means you can screw the seat on from inside the frame later instead of trying to reach up from underneath a finished stool. The stretchers get an extra trick — a pocket hole from two different faces on each 2x2, which locks the joint against racking in both directions instead of just one.

tools.note: For rounding or shaping the backrest top edge, if you want a softer profile than a straight cut.

cutList.note: Extend up to form the backrest posts.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: The cut list names every part simply Pine, so sort it by cross-section before you touch the saw. From the 8 ft 1x2 cut four backrest slats at 8", two backrest sides at 7-1/4" and one backrest top rail at 15". From the 3 ft 1x3 cut two backrest apron pieces at 15". From the 1x4 cut two front aprons at 15", two side aprons at 16", five seat slats at 18" and eight seat slat end caps at 3-1/2". From the 2x2 cut two back legs at 45", two front legs at 29-1/4", two side stretchers at 15" and two front/back stretchers at 16". Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, the 1x2, 1x3 and 1x4 all come out of what the materials list declares — one 8 ft 1x2, one 3 ft 1x3 and two 8 ft 1x4 — but the 2x2 parts total 210-1/2", which cannot come out of two 8 ft boards however you arrange them, so buy three. Square every cut and label each piece; the 15" and 16" pieces appear in both the aprons and the stretchers and are easy to mix up.

steps.title: Build the backrest
steps.body: The backrest is a small frame you build flat on the bench and add to the stool later: the 15" 1x2 top rail across the top, a 7-1/4" 1x2 side under each end, and the four 8" 1x2 slats spanning between them, spaced evenly across the 15" width. Drill 3/4" pocket holes and join it with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue — the 2-1/2" screws in the materials list would blow straight out through the face of 3/4" stock and are reserved for the 2x2 stretchers later. If you want a softer profile than a square top, shape the upper edge of the top rail with a jigsaw now, while the frame is still off the stool and easy to clamp. Check it square across the diagonals before the glue sets; this assembly ends up at eye level and any twist in it is the first thing anyone sees.

steps.title: Build the seat frame
steps.body: Stand the two 45" back legs and the two 29-1/4" front legs up and join them with the 1x4 aprons: the two 15" front aprons front and back, the two 16" side aprons side to side, all on edge and all set at the same height so the seat lands flat. Before you attach any of them, drill 3/4" pocket holes along the top inside edge of every apron facing UPWARD, as well as the usual holes in the ends for joining apron to leg — the upward holes are what let you screw the seat down from inside the frame later instead of lying under a finished stool trying to drive a screw overhead. Fasten with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue; the aprons are 3/4" stock and a 2-1/2" screw would burst out through the outside face of a 1-1/2" leg. Check both diagonals across the top of the frame and confirm the two back legs stand parallel, since they carry the backrest. If the stool feels like it wants more, add corner braces now while you can still reach inside.

steps.title: Add the stretchers
steps.body: The four 2x2 stretchers tie the legs together lower down at footrest height — set them level all the way round and at the same height on both sides, measuring down from the underside of the aprons rather than up from the floor. Note before you drill that the stretcher lengths are the mirror of the aprons above: the aprons are 15" front and back and 16" side to side, while the cut list gives 15" side stretchers and 16" front/back stretchers. Dry-fit each stretcher in the gap it is supposed to span and use whichever one actually fits, rather than trusting the label. These are the only 1-1/2"-into-1-1/2" joints on the stool, so this is where the 2-1/2" pocket screws belong; drill 1-1/2" pocket holes for them, and drill into two different faces of each stretcher end rather than twice into the same face, which locks the joint against racking in both directions. That is eight stretcher ends, so at two screws each you need sixteen 2-1/2" screws against the eight the materials list declares.

steps.title: Add the seat
steps.body: Lay the five 18" 1x4 seat slats across the frame — five boards at 3-1/2" wide give 17-1/2" of solid seat — and shuffle them until the gaps between them are even and the outer two sit where you want them relative to the frame's outside faces. Screw each slat down from underneath through the upward-facing apron pocket holes you drilled earlier, using 1-1/4" pocket screws, and go easy with the glue so a cracked slat can be replaced later without destroying the frame. The cut list also gives eight 3-1/2" seat slat end caps, but no step in the source says where they go, so leave them aside and check the photo rather than fastening them somewhere they were never meant to be. Sit on the stool before you go any further: a slat that flexes is telling you about a screw you missed.

steps.title: Mount the backrest
steps.body: The two 45" back legs stand 15-3/4" above the 29-1/4" front legs, and that projecting length is what the backrest frame mounts to — the source builds the backrest but never tells you to attach it, so do it here. Its 15" top rail is the same span the front and back aprons set between the legs, so the frame should sit between the two back legs with its sides against their inside faces; clamp it in place, check it is level and parallel to the seat, then fasten through 3/4" pocket holes with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue. Set the height by sitting on the stool and finding where it lands comfortably against your back, since the source does not fix it. The two 15" 1x3 backrest apron pieces are the same 15" span again and clearly belong to this assembly, but no step places them — dry-fit them against the photo before committing.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every edge smooth, easing the seat and backrest edges especially — this is a seat someone sits against for a while. Finish as desired.

images.alt: Vintage-style wooden bar stool with a slatted seat and low backrest
